title: Building With Claude Code
subtitle: A practical guide to getting what you actually want — systems you can use without me
author: William Marceau — Marceau Solutions
content_markdown:
## The Core Problem (Plain English)

Everything we've built lives inside a codebase that requires VS Code and Claude Code to navigate. That's backwards. The tools should serve you — not require you to summon an AI every time you need to use them.

Here's the pattern that's been happening:

- You describe what you want to build
- I build it as a Python script or CLI tool
- You ask how to use it
- I tell you to run `python some/deep/path/script.py --flag value`
- That command isn't memorable, isn't visual, and breaks whenever a path changes
- You come back to me to find it again

That's a dependency loop. **The goal is to break that loop.**

---

## The Right Interface for the Job

Before any tool gets built, the first question must be: **how will you actually use this?**

Most real systems need more than one layer. The key is matching each layer to the right tool:

| Layer | Build it as... | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduled automation (posting, data capture, alerts) | **n8n workflow** | Runs without you. You control it from the n8n UI — activate, deactivate, edit, test. |
| Dashboard, decision-making interface, content management | **Standalone web app at a fixed URL** | Opens in browser, no terminal required. Bookmarkable. |
| Notifications and alerts | **SMS/email via Twilio/n8n** | Comes to you. You don't go to it. |
| Guides, reports, reference documents | **Branded PDF, auto-opened** | Delivered, not buried in a file path. |
| One-time setup or developer utility | **CLI script** | Acceptable — but must have a plain README next to it. |

**Most real systems are hybrid.** The table above shows individual layers, but in practice you'll often need two or three working together. The question isn't *"should this be an n8n workflow or a web app?"* — it's *"which parts run automatically, which parts do I need to see, and which parts need to alert me?"* A product launch system needs n8n posting on a schedule AND a web dashboard showing results AND SMS alerts when milestones hit. Plan for the combination from the start — not as an afterthought when the first layer isn't enough.

**The rule:** If using the tool requires opening VS Code, it's built wrong.

---

## The LaunchPad Example — What Went Wrong and Why

The iterative launch system needed to be **hybrid from the start** — the mistake wasn't building a web app, it was building the CLI as the entry point to everything.

The correct breakdown for a system like this:

- **n8n handles** the scheduled posting, API calls, and data capture — runs automatically on a schedule, you control it from the n8n dashboard
- **The web dashboard handles** displaying what n8n collected so you can make go/no-go decisions — always-on at a fixed URL

**What should have happened from the beginning:**
1. You say: *"I want a system that posts content to social on a schedule and shows me a dashboard with my waitlist count and go/no-go metrics"*
2. I say: *"That's two layers — an n8n automation layer and a web dashboard on top of the data it collects. Here's the n8n workflow to activate, and here's the dashboard URL."*
3. No CLI. No menus. Two interfaces, each used independently.

The question to ask at the start of any build isn't *"n8n or web app?"* — it's *"what needs to run automatically, and what do I need to see and interact with?"* Those are often different layers of the same system.

---

## How to Describe What You Want (The Prompting Framework)

The quality of what gets built is almost entirely determined by the quality of the initial description. Work through these four steps before starting any build.

### Step 1 — Lead With the Output

Don't describe the system. Describe what you're holding in your hands when it's done.

**Weak:** *"Build me an automated iterative product launch system"*

**Strong:** *"I want a web page I can open in my browser that shows me: which platforms I've posted to, my waitlist signup count with a progress bar toward 100, and a download button for each post's content package. Each card should show me the copy so I don't have to go looking for it. The posting itself should happen automatically on a schedule I set in n8n."*

The second version tells me exactly what to build because it describes the *experience* of using the thing — and it distinguishes the automation layer from the visibility layer.

### Step 2 — Specify Every Interface Layer

A system can have multiple layers. Name each one explicitly:

- *"The automation should be an n8n workflow I can activate/deactivate"*
- *"The dashboard should open in my browser at localhost"*
- *"Send me a text message / email when X happens"*
- *"Output a PDF at the end"*
- *"This piece should run automatically on a schedule"*

If you don't specify, I'll default to whatever is technically easiest — which is usually a CLI script.

### Step 3 — Name the User

- *"I need to use this from my phone"*
- *"A PT client needs to fill this out"*
- *"I'm posting this on social media"*
- *"A future customer of the SaaS will use this"*

This changes everything about how each layer gets built.

### Step 4 — Say What Already Exists

Reference what we've built before when it's relevant. This prevents rebuilding things.

- *"We have a video ad generator in execution/video_ads.py — use that for the content"*
- *"The n8n waitlist capture webhook is already live — just read from that Sheet"*
- *"We have a branded PDF engine — output this as a PDF, not a markdown file"*

---

### Putting It All Together — The Prompt Template

The four steps above map directly to this template. Copy it when starting any new build:

---

*"When this is done, I should be able to [Step 1 — describe the experience: open a URL and see X, receive a text when Y happens, download a PDF with Z].*

*The interfaces should be [Step 2 — list each layer: n8n for X, web dashboard for Y, SMS for Z].*

*The user is [Step 3 — me / a client / a SaaS customer].*

*We already have [Step 4 — existing tools/scripts/workflows] — use those.*

*Research phase: [see below — what you found, what's still unclear].*

*Success looks like: [concrete description of working output]."*

---

## The Research Phase — Collaborative, Not Solo

For complex new domains, research before building produces dramatically better results. This is a **two-part collaborative process** — not something you do alone.

### Part 1 — Parallel Research (before the session)

**You research** the domain:
- Terminology you don't fully know yet
- What the standard workflow looks like in this space
- What tools or platforms others use for this
- Any known platform limitations (e.g. "TikTok unaudited apps can only post privately")

**I research** the technical landscape:
- What's already in our stack (`execution/`, n8n workflows, existing scripts)
- API capabilities and rate limits
- Known gotchas and failure modes
- What would need to be built vs. what already exists

**Useful searches for your side:**
- `[topic] best practices 2024/2025`
- `[topic] SaaS examples`
- `how does [platform] API work`
- `[platform] API limitations`

### Part 2 — Clarification Q&A (start of the session)

Before any code is written, we run an interactive Q&A to align:

- What you learned and what's still unclear
- What I found in the stack that's already usable
- Any terminology gaps or wrong assumptions on either side
- Final agreement on architecture — layers, interfaces, data flow — before building starts

This session is short (10–15 min) but it prevents building the wrong thing entirely.

**Skip the research phase if:**
- You're describing a business process you already do manually
- You're adding a feature to something we've already built
- The request is purely operational ("run the SMS campaign", "deploy the website")

---

## Finding What We've Built

The master index of everything is `docs/SYSTEM-STATE.md`. Open it first when you're looking for something.

Any marketing asset for a specific product should live at `projects/marceau-solutions/labs/[product]/marketing/` and be listed in that product's CLAUDE.md. **Every asset gets documented in its project's CLAUDE.md the moment it's created.**

**Quick lookup commands:**

| What you're looking for | How to find it |
|---|---|
| A script or tool | `python scripts/inventory.py search [keyword]` |
| System/infra state | `docs/SYSTEM-STATE.md` |
| A specific project | Its `CLAUDE.md` file |
| Recent session work | `docs/session-history.md` |
| API keys | `.env` at the root |

---

## The Deletion Problem

Things have been deleted or moved without clear documentation. This was a failure of process. The new rules:

- **Nothing in `labs/` or `projects/` gets deleted** without your explicit confirmation
- **Generated outputs** (PDFs, ad creatives, research) get **moved** to the right location, never deleted
- **Before any cleanup**, I produce a list of what will be removed and you approve it
- **Marketing assets** always land in `[project]/marketing/` with a human-readable name like `flyer-qr-code.png`, not `output_v3_final.png`

---

## The Dependency Trap — How to Avoid It

Every new system should pass this test: **Can you use it in 6 months without remembering how we built it?**

If the answer is no, we haven't finished building it yet. The final step of any build is:

1. A plain-English README or CLAUDE.md in the project folder
2. One command or URL to launch each interface layer
3. Documentation of what it does and how to interact with it
4. The output format (where does the result go?)

**The n8n principle:** n8n is the right model for automation layers. You can see the workflow, activate it, test it, and modify it without ever talking to me. When something runs on a schedule or reacts to a trigger, build it in n8n.

---

## Rules Going Forward

These are now in the system. Any new build follows them automatically:

1. **Interface-first** — before writing a line of code, confirm how you'll access the result
2. **Match layers to tools** — n8n for automation, web app for dashboards, SMS/email for alerts; complex systems often need all three
3. **Direct launch scripts** for every web app — one command to start it, browser opens
4. **Nothing deleted without approval** — move, don't delete
5. **Every output documented** — file created → documented in that project's CLAUDE.md immediately
6. **PDF for guides** — any instructions, educational content, or reference material is a branded PDF you can open and read
7. **Research phase is collaborative** — for complex new domains, we both research in parallel, then run a clarification Q&A before any code is written

---

## The Bigger Picture

The goal you described — building a company on a laptop — is achievable. The stack you have is real and powerful: n8n for automation, Twilio for SMS, the video ad tools, the AI content pipeline, the website infrastructure. None of that has to be re-explained to me every session.

The shift that needs to happen is moving from *building things I can run* to *building things either of us can run*. Every session should leave you with something you can go operate on your own — and leave me with enough context that the next Claude session can pick it up without re-explaining everything. Both sides of that equation matter. If only you can use it, I'll rebuild it wrong next time. If only I can use it, you're stuck waiting for me.

Use this guide as a checklist at the start of any new build. The better you describe what you want before I start, the closer the first version will be to what you actually need.
